timelines.notes: Fetched 2026-05-11. Aggregated across Al Jazeera's Kashmir-region coverage (per user instruction). Scoped Kashmir-specifically to differentiate from c2_a_operation_sindoor: this timeline focuses on Kashmir-region events (LoC firing, internal unrest, demolitions in Kashmir, civilian impact, separatist politics) rather than the bilateral diplomatic/strike track which sits in c2_a. Contributing articles: https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/8/1/new-wave-why-suspected-rebel-attacks-are-spiking-in-kashmirs-jammu-area (source for all four 2024 Jammu entries) ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/7/two-indian-soldiers-six-rebels-killed-in-kashmir-gun-battles ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/7/16/at-least-4-indian-soldiers-killed-during-firefight-in-kashmir ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/liveblog/2025/4/23/kashmir-attack-live-india-looks-for-gunmen-after-26-killed-in-pahalgam ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/4/24/pakistan-announces-retaliatory-measures-against-india-after-kashmir-attack ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/liveblog/2025/4/26/kashmir-attack-live-india-pakistan-trade-fire-as-pahalgam-gunmen-hunted ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/liveblog/2025/4/27/kashmir-attack-live-india-pakistan-troops-exchange-fire-for-third-day ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/4/28/india-and-pakistan-continue-to-trade-fire-across-kashmir-border ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/5/2/pahalgam-attack-a-simple-guide-to-the-kashmir-conflict (mostly historical so most entries are out-of-window — used for context only) ; https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2025/5/2/everyone-lives-in-fear-voices-of-kashmir-after-deadly-pahalgam-attack ; https://www.aljazeera.com/gallery/2025/5/12/india-pakistan-and-the-fight-for-kashmir ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/5/12/we-want-permanent-solution-fear-and-fatigue-in-kashmir-after-ceasefire ; https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2025/10/2/yasin-malik-kashmirs-best-known-separatist-an-indian-intelligence-asset ; https://www.aljazeera.com/features/2025/12/4/whats-our-fault-indias-expulsion-of-pakistanis-still-splits-families . Articles considered but dropped per rule 5 (out-of-window): https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2024/5/14/unrest-in-pakistani-kashmir-whats-behind-the-recent-wave-of-protests (May 2024 — before July 1 cluster start). Date approximations per rule 4: July 27, 2024 (Kupwara infiltration) inferred — AJ Aug 1 piece described event as 'Saturday' before the Thursday publication, making July 27 the most defensible date; July 16, 2024 (Doda) — AJ Aug 1 piece said 'a week later' after July 8, cross-referenced to AJ's standalone July 16 piece. Apr 23 (Kashmir Resistance claim of responsibility) and Apr 25 (demolitions begin) framed from AJ's liveblog coverage, which timestamps these as the days following the attack. Source disagreements: India and Pakistan dispute responsibility for LoC firing — AJ entries on Apr 26/27 attribute exchange-of-fire reports to both militaries' competing claims, captured neutrally. Yasin Malik (Oct 2025): the named subject is a publicly identified separatist leader currently in Indian custody — included per rule 6 since he is a public figure of record; the underlying allegation that he was an Indian intelligence asset comes from AJ's investigative piece, which both Indian agencies and Malik's family contest.

Fetched 2026-05-11. Aggregated across NPR's Kashmir-region coverage (per user instruction). Scoped Kashmir-specifically per same reasoning as t1. Contributing articles: https://www.npr.org/2024/09/18/g-s1-23576/kashmir-election-india (only substantial NPR pre-Pahalgam piece in cluster window covering Kashmir politics) ; https://www.npr.org/2025/04/23/nx-s1-5373353/kashmir-mass-shooting-india-vance-trump-modi ; https://www.npr.org/2025/04/23/nx-s1-5373960/india-pakistan-tensions-flare-up-following-deadly-attack-in-kashmir ; https://www.npr.org/2025/04/24/g-s1-62469/india-pakistan-kashmir-attack-tensions ; https://www.npr.org/2025/04/25/g-s1-62677/india-pakistan-kashmir-line-of-control-firing ; https://www.npr.org/2025/04/27/nx-s1-5378630/india-kashmir-pakistan-crackdown-homes-destroyed ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/06/nx-s1-5388952/india-attacks-pakistan ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/07/nx-s1-5389777/tensions-escalate-as-pakistan-calls-indias-operation-an-act-of-war ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/08/g-s1-64959/india-pakistan-attacks ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/08/nx-s1-5389651/india-pakistan-conflict-international-community ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/11/g-s1-65755/india-pakistan-ceasefire-shaken-overnight-fighting-kashmir ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/11/nx-s1-5392008/a-closer-look-at-the-history-of-the-conflict-in-kashmir (mostly historical — used for context only) ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/12/g-s1-65967/india-pakistan-kashmir-ceasefire ; https://www.npr.org/2025/05/22/g-s1-67862/kashmir-attack-india-pakistan-deportations ; https://www.npr.org/2025/07/30/nx-s1-5484831/india-militants-killed-kashmir ; https://www.npr.org/2025/10/01/nx-s1-5552099/muslims-in-india-pay-the-price-for-april-militant-attack-in-kashmir . Date approximations per rule 4: April 30, 2025 (Pakistani departures) — NPR's May 22 piece said 'by the end of April — the deadline for Pakistanis to depart India — local media reported that more than 780 Pakistani nationals had left'; April 30 chosen as the most defensible specific date for that deadline. May 7 (strikes hit Kashmir) — NPR's May 6/7 piece reported strikes happened overnight; May 7 chosen as the date the action was publicly reported and casualties recorded. Source disagreements: Casualty figures from May 7 strikes differ between sources — NPR's piece notes seven killed in Indian-controlled Kashmir per AP, while Pakistan stated 31 dead on Pakistan side; entry on May 7 captures NPR's reporting on Kashmir casualties only. India denied targeting civilian infrastructure; Pakistan denied hosting militants — both denials noted in respective NPR pieces but not given separate dated entries. Family-name redactions in NPR's May 22 piece (the Bandipora family) followed per rule 6 — no private individuals named. Public officials (Modi, Mehbooba Mufti, etc.) and the public figure Yasin Malik (not on this NPR timeline) appear by name in linked NPR coverage and are permitted.
